title: OpenAI Staffers Are Funding a Rival Super PAC to Take on Their Boss
gist: At least eight current and former OpenAI employees have donated over $215,000 to Guardrails Alliance, a pro-regulation super PAC, to counter Leading the Future, the $100M+ pro-AI-industry PAC backed by OpenAI president Greg Brockman -- exposing an internal rift at OpenAI over AI political spending just as Anthropic backs its own $20M counter-PAC.

themes.theme: OpenAI employee dissent on AI policy

insights.claim: At least eight current and former OpenAI employees have collectively donated more than $215,000 to Guardrails Alliance, a super PAC pushing for stricter regulation of frontier AI labs, directly opposing their own company's president.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: Internal fracture at a leading AI lab over its own political spending signals reputational and governance risk that could affect OpenAI's regulatory positioning and talent retention.
insights.quote: A group of rank-and-file OpenAI employees have donated more than $215,000 to a super PAC pushing for stricter regulations on frontier AI labs.

insights.claim: OpenAI research engineer Juan Felipe Cerón Uribe donated $200,000 to Guardrails Alliance -- the largest employee gift -- saying that after four years working on OpenAI's AI-harm-mitigation strategy he feared that research would be wasted without external guardrails holding private companies accountable.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: A senior internal safety researcher publicly doubting that self-regulation works is a leading indicator that OpenAI's internal safety commitments may not be viewed as sufficient even by its own staff.
insights.quote: Tech billionaires, such as Greg Brockman, funded the super PAC Leading the Future to keep AI unregulated. I was very happy to learn that Guardrails Alliance is pushing back against LTF; my decision to donate to them was easy.
insights.locator: One of the largest donations from an OpenAI employee

insights.claim: Beyond the single largest donor, several current OpenAI alignment and safety researchers -- Gabriel Wu, Julie Steele, and Jason Wolfe -- plus former seven-year OpenAI research manager David Farhi, each gave between $3,000 and $5,000 to Guardrails Alliance, showing the dissent spans multiple safety teams rather than being an isolated incident.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: Breadth of safety-researcher donors across teams suggests systemic internal tension at OpenAI over political strategy, worth monitoring for further departures or public dissent.
insights.locator: Gabriel Wu, an OpenAI safety researcher, says he gave $5,000

themes.theme: Funding imbalance in the AI-regulation super PAC war

insights.claim: Leading the Future, the pro-AI-industry super PAC backed by OpenAI president Greg Brockman, has raised more than $100 million including a $50 million joint commitment from Brockman and his wife Anna, dwarfing Guardrails Alliance's $15 million fundraising target for the election cycle.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: Quantifies the capital asymmetry in AI lobbying; investors tracking AI-regulation odds should weight Leading the Future's ~7x fundraising advantage heavily against grassroots opposition.
insights.quote: a pro-AI industry super PAC bankrolled with more than $100 million from technology industry leaders, including OpenAI president and cofounder Greg Brockman

insights.claim: Guardrails Alliance cofounder Shaunna Thomas argues her group does not need to match Leading the Future dollar-for-dollar because publicizing AI-industry PAC spending mobilizes public opinion more cheaply than the industry's own spending can buy support.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: Tests whether an asymmetric, opinion-driven campaign can offset a 7x capital disadvantage in AI policy races -- a signal for whether grassroots pro-regulation efforts can meaningfully slow AI industry lobbying.
insights.quote: When you expose what the AI PACs are doing, the people reject it. We're leveraging public opinion that already exists, and it's less expensive to do that.

themes.theme: Leading the Future's electoral track record and industry split

insights.claim: Leading the Future's first major target was New York state legislator Alex Bores, author of New York's landmark AI safety law, whose congressional primary drew $27 million in combined pro-AI and pro-safeguard spending and who ultimately lost.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: Demonstrates AI-industry PACs' proven capacity to defeat a sitting pro-regulation legislator in a primary, a bellwether for whether similar AI safety legislation elsewhere can survive well-funded industry opposition.
insights.locator: Among the PAC's first political efforts

insights.claim: Anthropic has committed $20 million to back Public First Action, a separate super PAC promoting AI safeguards and opposing pro-industry groups in the 2026 elections, placing Anthropic and OpenAI's leadership on opposite sides of competing political action committees.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: A visible regulatory-strategy split between the two leading frontier labs could become a competitive and reputational differentiator, and signals Anthropic is willing to spend meaningfully to shape AI policy outcomes.
insights.locator: Guardrails Alliance isn't the first PAC trying to combat Leading the Future

insights.claim: OpenAI has publicly framed Brockman's involvement with Leading the Future as a personal-capacity activity unrelated to the company, even though OpenAI's global affairs chief Chris Lehane has said he helped set up the PAC and regularly consults with Brockman on his political giving.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: The gap between OpenAI's 'personal capacity' framing and its global affairs chief's admitted role in setting up the PAC is a reputational and disclosure risk to monitor if scrutiny of AI industry lobbying increases.
insights.quote: employees are free to participate in the political process in their personal capacities, including by donating or providing advice to candidates, campaigns, and political organizations
insights.locator: OpenAI spokesperson pointed WIRED to a June blog post

themes.theme: Cracks in venture-capital consensus on unregulated AI

insights.claim: John O'Farrell, Andreessen Horowitz's first outside partner (joined 2010, left the firm in 2026), is expected to be disclosed as a Guardrails Alliance donor and has publicly accused his former colleagues of using Leading the Future to intimidate politicians who engage seriously with AI governance.
insights.speaker: Maxwell Zeff
insights.so_what: A former top-tier VC partner breaking ranks to fund the opposition and publicly criticize a16z-adjacent political tactics is an early indicator that elite VC consensus favoring unregulated AI development is not unanimous.
insights.quote: intimidate politicians who appear to engage too aggressively with the question of how to govern A.I.
insights.locator: O'Farrell was the first outside partner to join Andreessen Horowitz
